speaker: interviewer
text: --Koby. Tape number 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Mr.-- the-- the man's name that found us in his barn was Mr. Daniluk, Vasilii Daniluk, like Basil Daniluk. And his nickname was [? Kobza. ?] I don't know why it was [? Kobza, ?] but that's what they used to call him. Big man. He went home. And about an hour-- he loaded up. He took a lot of hay with him. He came back with pots and pans of hot food and water. He even brought two apples. With him, with Mr. Daniluk, came his son, who was, during the war or right before the war, was a soldier in the Polish Army. Naturally he got captured by the Germans and was a prisoner of war in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the time and-- and at Christmastime of '42, the Germans let him come home for vacation to visit his family. He came with his father, the two of them, OK? I didn't mention it that they-- both of them found us. That's why we knew all this nice information about North Africa and Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came and we-- he even brought wooden spoons. You know, they had those cast iron pots. That's what people used then. And he brought us wooden spoons and this-- all this delicious food and bread and cookies, you know, big peasant cookies. You know, it was just like a big swollen pizza. And we had this food and the apple. And he said, you can stay all you want. As soon as they left and they closed the barn, there was-- they had a conference, my father, Uncle Chaim, and my mother. And they made a-- just had a conference. And the decision was, no matter what, we got to leave here. We can't stay. The decision was made. They had no advance notice. They did not make any arrangements for, you know, for another place. And we had to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally decided that the-- down the road towards the village-- there's still a-- this is the suburbs, very few houses-- there's a barn. We're going to go into that barn. But they figured out that the man was not too reliable-- nervous, panicky man. If he finds us, there's going to be a problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there that same-- same night, 1, 2 o'clock. Developed a-- a wind. It was cold. This was the winter-- first week in January or second week in January. Cold like the dickens. We got in that barn, and the barn was made out of like a-- [? woven ?] out of reeds-- you know, weeds and sticks, you know, branches of young trees. It was [? woven ?] like a-- like a basket, you know, you get from South America or from China. But this was a big place, a big barn. And we got in there, and there was-- you could not fall asleep. It was a drafty place. It was on a hill. And the wind, you know, I guess when it goes from the valley up, went right through that barn, and you almost froze to death. We walked around, got in line on the threshing floor, and we walked we're so cold. Did not dare to fall asleep. We spent the-- the night there. The next morning, we stayed there. And the meantime, my father went away to find a new place of hiding. And he couldn't come back, so he had to stay over the day there to come back the next night. He came back. And it was already a weekday, was not a holiday. And about 2, 3 o'clock in the afternoon, guess who comes to the barn with-- they didn't-- or the-- the man that owns the barn comes there with a team of horses. And he opens up the barn, but surprise-- surprises us right on the threshing floor. He start hollering and screaming, Jews in my barn. And my-- my father and my uncle grabbed him. They knew him. And they say, come on in. Be quiet. Don't make-- you know, you're going to kill us and yourself too. Somehow he quiet down. They promised him they're going to disappear tonight. He's praying. He got down on his knees.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left. My father found a place with this Mr. Stakh-- Mr. Stakh Denishchuk. You know, you know Polish word 'Stach,' Stanley? That's the man. Stach-- Stakh, that's a nice Ukrainian name, Stakh, that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he found a-- he has a place for us, and we're going to go there tonight. You know, when the snow is, who know, places 12 feet, 6 feet, 10 feet, and you have to have a hike of maybe 7, 8 kilometers, because you have to go outside the village. You don't want to go through the village in the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get there. And where we going to be? We're going to be on the-- in the attic of a pigsty. But it-- it's not completely ready. Only part of the attic is full with straw. You know, because they put for the winter straw to act-- to act as an insulator. So we get in. We stayed in it about three or four days in one side. We finally arranged to have the straw for the other side. But he would not-- so we were sitting [? on ?] that-- over there already. So there was my father, my mother, Uncle Chaim, my brother Bob, and myself. We got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they loaded the straw. But Stakh went in there, say, hey, you boys, hand over the straw, and I'll push it around here. And they filled up the-- the loft, the attic, with straw, and that's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stakh-- the pigs-- they were-- you know, the pigs belonged to the Germans. It's a big estate. The people could have stolen them. But, you know, somebody had to be responsible. Where are your pigs, you know? You stole them. But I thought about it. They would have stolen a half a dozen pigs. They would of-- could had a picnic, the whole village. But they didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in charge. He was the foreman to make sure that the pigs were in good shape, well fed, and so [? the Germans ?] going to [INAUDIBLE] them. But he had three helpers. He had four helpers. There were three men and a girl-- and a lady. I don't know who the lady was, and I didn't know who the other men-- one man was. But I knew the other two men. One was Olga's-- our next door neighbor's brother, who was older-- I used to rent the skis from him when I was [? free-- ?] and his uncle. His uncle was not-- he was a little bit mentally incapacitated. So there- so you have four people working there. They fed the pigs and the water and [? where ?] they clean. And Stakh was our guardian angel. When they used to send him home, sometimes they used to boil-- most of the time they used to boil the potatoes. They had a big kettle. And Stakh-- and when they went, he say, OK, you guys can go home. They went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to take, you know, a couple more. He said, ah, there's one pig not fed? I'll take care of it. And he used to bring a bucket of potatoes and hand it to-- the-- the potatoes were intended for the pigs-- and he used to hand it up to my father and a pail of water. The water's terrible. They used to get the water-- there was a-- a flour mill. It was like a block away. They used to bring that metallic water to feed the pigs with. We had to drink it because he couldn't go get fresh water. Tasted-- you know, it went through the engine with the oil. It was water-cooled engines. Ach, what are you going to do? You tasted, you know, flavored water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes, we had galore. Once in a while, we used to get baked potatoes with the coals on them yet, because, you know, you had to work at them fast. And we stayed there, I don't know, about a month. Uncle Chaim decided it's not for him. He's abandoning us. He's got a place where they're going to take good care of him. Well, it turned out that Uncle Chaim had money. I think he bought presents for the-- you know, he gave them the money, and they took him in or whatever. But he had a place like a resort. He had a nice attic to himself with a nice wooden floor, smooth like your table. Three meals a day, a bath twice a week, maybe once a week. But he looked nice and clean with rosy cheek. I visited him once. He almost had me killed. But I'm sorry. I forgave him a while ago, because just died recently. So you got-- the-- the dead ones you got to forgive. And he went away over there. But they had a sister. My father and Uncle Chaim had a sister hidden not far from where Uncle Chaim was-- was hidden. Well, the hero of the expedition was my mother. She went to find out, you know, how Aunt-- Aunt Chaika is surviving. She came there, and she came back with Aunt Chaika. Aunt Chaika must have weighed 150, maybe 170, a big woman, bigger than my father. And my father was no slouch, and [? here ?] a bigger woman. So I don't know how old she was, but she was a big lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you get-- imagine to get the woman up to the-- through that little hole up into the attic? She came, and she stayed with us for a while. She stayed with us for the rest of the expedition, till we were liberated. One day sometimes in-- I don't know, July, August, sometimes in the summer, Stakh came and told us that we have to leave, because there are rumors that the freedom fighters are going burn down the estate. It's up to you. You want to stay, you can stay. You-- you want to get fried? [INAUDIBLE]. A little side-- a side story to that. The Germans used to come and inspect, you know, the estate to see whatever. Naturally most of them were officers, right? You don't send a-- a plain soldier. He's needed on the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were ladies in the thing and in the village. One of the ladies was the daughter of the deacon of the church. Well, this German officer-- you got to-- you know, lovers have to find a place somewhere. Well, they ended up in the pigsty. We were upstairs, you know, and the ceiling was no more than 6 feet from-- you know, pigs don't need fancy places. They need halfway decent shelter. And he brought this young lady with him, and I guess they did a stand-up job. It was-- you know, we were lying there, and we almost died from lying quiet. No one-- when they left, no one said a word, and there were my parents, my Aunt Chaika, my brother. To this day, we never discussed that particular incident. Well, that was a side story to that little Stakh's adventure. We left Stakh. We got to go somewheres. So we decided to go cross country to another village, to a Czech. The Czech already had a-- his name was Mr. Zumer, a womanizer and a alcoholic at the same time. Had a-- his father was still alive. He inherited a beautiful estate-- lots of beautiful fields, barns, horses, cattle, a wife and six kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he had one of [? those-- ?] and he used to socialize with the Germans a lot, because he's a-- he was an intellectual, you see? He went to school in Czechoslovakia before the war. So he says, I'm an intellectual. He used to say to my father, says, Kobylanski, don't forget, [NON-ENGLISH], an intellectual. So father says, well, big deal. I don't know what my father said.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he already-- among the Jews he had already a bad reputation that he's a killer. He used to betray whatever Jews were hidden. He used to tell the Germans, and they did their job. Well, we went to him because my father trusted him. We got there. He put us up in his former pigeon cove. The pigeon cove was above the pigsty, but this was a nice place. You know, they had-- Czechs were wealthy compared to the rest of the population, to the native Ukrainians. There were well off-- nice homes, nice estates. Well, the pigeon cove was not so good because it's in the summer, and it was covered with tin roofs. And you know, tin roofs get-- have a habit of getting hot. We almost died of heat over there. But in addition to the heat, there were fleas. And they used to jump like-- you know, like a bullet ricochets or you throw a stone, it bounces back and-- like those Super Balls, you know, it bounces back and-- that's how those fleas move. And they bite, too. Ach, god almighty, terrible. We cleaned out all the garbage from the pigeons and all the stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife used to take care of us. We had a meal every day, but, you know, only one. They had a big pot of soup, a vegetable soup, [? rice. ?] You want a slice of bread or you need water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forget about bath or washing up. I had one bath from September of '42 till February of 1944. So did my mother. No one else. We are the one-- the only ones that got a bath once. Maybe we had two baths in one week. That was luxury. And we stayed there with him for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something happened, and he didn't show up for a week. He didn't show up for two weeks, for three weeks, for four weeks. And we decided, my parents decided, that that's not a good omen. Who knows? He might be somewheres on a-- a drinking binge, and he'll brag-- brag to somebody he has Jews in his pigeon cove. Who's going to-- who's going to stop him? So we decided, they decided, to move. Next door to Mr.-- this is a little side story to the-- next door to Mr. Zumer lived Czechs, Czechs with the same set up, you know, pro-German and anti-German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Majority of them were anti-German. I mean, let's say they were neutral. And if they were anti-German, they did not say. Neutral. They carried on their hard work and productivity like nothing happened. But there were those that were pro-German openly and hated Jews. I don't know why, but that's the way it all goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to this pigsty, to this pigeon cove, there was a beautiful pear tree, and you could see it during the day. It was Bartlett pears, beautiful. [INAUDIBLE] No, they're like-- not Bartlett, those long ones. But they look similar to Bartlett. We'll call them Bartlett pears. And they were beauties hanging from that tree. Oh, how do you get a hold of one? [? tonight? ?] Delicious, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day-- I used to be in the quartermaster corps. You know, if we were short on food, I used to go already if they-- parents picked out two to go to. We only went to one house at a time. You know, figure you go to two houses in one night, you'll pick up twice as much. No, only one house. You could go to only one place, and whatever you got, that was it. You come back. Coming back, I climbed up the tree, and I must have taken off maybe a dozen pears. I got up into to the pigeon cove, and my parents found out that I picked off these pears. They were like outside the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they told me, you are not trustworthy. You shouldn't have done that. The man is going to become suspicious, because he probably-- he'll notice that the pears missing and-- I thought that was crazy. How would he know how many pears are missing? Guess what? On Sunday-- I think two days later or three days-- on Sunday he comes around and he looks at the pear, and he screams, Jews stole my pears. And we can hear him. And he's raving mad, and he says, the goddamn Jews stole my pears. He says, argh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went to the house, and a couple of his sons came over to look at the pear, because you got to pay attention to your father. And he was raving mad. I thought, oh, god, I begin to understand. It finally dawned on me-- he-- you know, who knows what he could do. So we saw-- that was the story. But I never did it again. No, once-- once I did one another thing. I went to the people that lived next door to us for food. And on the way back-- it was all during the night in the summer-- I-- going back to our hiding place, it was a different-- and I came across, you know, the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, everybody has its own garden. And I was walking barefoot. In the summer, you don't need shoes. And I came across a cucumber patch at night. You know, cucumbers, the vines and of-- cucumber have those little prickly things. You walk, you right away you figure out prickly? Cucumbers, oh. Pumpkins have the same kind, but they're a smaller scale. Ah, cucumbers, let's find. How can you find cucumbers at night without disturbing the vines over there? Oh, I lied down on my back, and I rolled. See, right away you find all the cucumbers, because the body tells where the-- but I don't know-- I don't know if anybody noticed it or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing is I picked the garden-- the poorest man in the village. We used to see him. When we were at Stakh's place, this man was-- lived the second house-- our house, Olga's house, and this man's house. We used to call him Mr. 'Tsibulka,' Mr. 'Onions,' because he used to brag-- he moved to our village from another area, from a different county where they used to raise onions. He used to brag about it. And that's why they called him Mr. 'Onion,' Mr. 'Tsibulka.' I don't remember his name. And we used to see him walk on the estate. He used to be the security guard there. [? Where ?] we were hidden, he used to work, you know, to work in the morning to whatever he did, the supervision there. His coat was patched. His pants were patches, you know, patches all over the place. The only thing that was one solid color on him were his boots, you know, the high boots up to the knee. He used to walk with a cane and smoke his cigarette. And my father said, that's what I want to be. I want to be a free man like him, not be afraid that I'm-- if I sneeze loud I'm going to die. Just like him. I don't know how much my father [? obeyed, ?] but I think he kept it in mind for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other places did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Mr. Zumer, when we decided that, you know, it's a bad place-- at the beginning, remember I told you my father went separately with my brother. And my mother went separate, and I went separately. Well, my mother was-- this Mr. Zumer lived in this village of Dombrowka. It's a, you know, it's like a Polish-Czech name, D-O-M-B-R-O-W-K-I-- K-A. It's like something oak-- oak ridge or oak village or something to do with that. And another one was a village, a Czech village, exclusively Czech, Martynowka. It was about maybe 3 miles, 3 miles at the most. My mother was hidden there at a man's place. His name was Mr. Dvorak. Mr. Dvorak had some kind of a defective hand, his left. His right hand was OK. His left, he always wore a sock on it. Not a glove, not a kind of a bandaid, but a sock. You could-- because you could see the heel. He always had a sock over it. I don't know what the problem was. But she was hidden to start with over there. And later, she gets to come cross country, come to visit her husband and us kids. And so we decided-- so they went over there. My mother and my father went there one night. We stayed at Zumer's. Don't forget Tante Chaika, the big woman with us. Well, she's going to be very helpful to me soon. And they went over there, and they talked with Mr. Dvorak. And he'll let us-- he'll take us in. He'll hide us. You know, they had big farms, big barns, lot of hay, a lot of straw from threshing. I don't know if it was by design or by accident or something, his big straw stack-- you know, when after threshing, you got to do something with it-- stood here, right by the barn. And he had a hiding place in there. You know what a fence, a fence, you know, they had wooden fences, maybe 4 feet, 5 feet tall. When they threw the straw, all the straw stayed on top of that fence. But the fence became a lean-to. So under this fence, there was a hole. He must have known about it. That's where we crawled into. And we stayed there the winter of '42. Ach, what a drafty place that was. It was also on a hill. So I think there's something to do with the air currents. And if it's a straight hole, it was terrible, and it was drafty. There was never a warm day there, never. He even had hired help. That's how well he was off, Mr. Dvorak. And Tante Chaika was with us, and I used to sleep next to Tante Chaika. So when she turned her back on me, it was toasty warm. When she turned the other way, I was cold. My brother slept with-- near my parents. It was always cold, always hungry, always thirsty, and always cold. And we stayed there the winter till about November, December, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one day, you know, he had hired help? But he had dogs also. One of the dogs got the smell of us, and he used to come to the hole and bark and bark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, we used to throw him food. You know, my father used to get to the hole there and throw him out a few pieces of bread. We had needed it for us, but to shut up this animal. [LAUGHS] So sometimes he used to go away on his own. He barked a few times. I think he got tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day, the help, the hired help man, got suspicious, and he followed the dog. Hey, you know, what's this? Called the dog Bobik. You know, [? well, ?] that's abbreviation. Bob, right? Bob. Bobik, Bobik, what you got there? You found something, a rabbit? Ah, he said, there won't be no rabbits here. He talked to the dog. Well, finally he got tired of talking to the dog, and these two went and got a pitchfork. Guess what? They found us. You know what you have to do. This is a Sunday, on a-- you have to disappear, because tomorrow, who knows? Well, they-- I-- I can imagine what my parents and Tante Chaika went through, actually were staring death in the face. Well, that night, they decided that they got to get out of here-- that day. But where do you go? So they went to Mr. Scastny, which was a block away from our house. I'll show you a picture of Mr. Scastny, his wife, and his two daughters. He also has a son. The deal was my father came back and he said, Mr. Scastny will take us in, but he has to give us a week to dig-- to get the shelter ready. Amazing. He had all these barns and all the stables. How long does it take to find a place for four, five Jews? They don't take up too much room. Seven days.

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought food that he used to feed the dog. No, I think he talked to the-- to feed the dog, and he used to-- he talked to the-- to the hired help. Well, Tante Chaika ran out of money. Tante Chaika had the money. My father didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: They paid him, you know, every day. You know, bribery or blackmail? They have today, you want 100. Tomorrow, you know, demand 200. And it had to last seven days. And finally, somehow we survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't last seven days. We went. It was a-- one of those windy days that blew away the snow from the fields. And snows are plowed, you know. They're not really made fine. You know, they're just big slabs. And the fields over there are not one big [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you have field-- property lines. And they're about half a foot wide between each field, because they probably put a marker and use the field. No. Everybody had about an 8-inch piece of land. That was the dividing line.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time you hit a-- you know, when they plowed, they plow away from the dividing line, right? So it's like a-- a sloping-- and you walk on that frozen stuff. And Tante Chaika's a big woman, and she holding on-- holding on to me. And when she falls, guess who she falls on. Not on the ground, she falls on me. Oh, boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Mr. Scastny. And we get there, and we figure it out what-- why it took seven days. He dug a hole for us in the pigsty. He took off the floor, because the Czechs kept their pigs on wooden floors. Cleanliness and, I guess, whatever, better productivity, less diseases, less death, of sudden death of pigs, or whatever. But they had all floors, and it's easier to clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he removed the floor. He dug a pit. It must have been below-- below the floor, 6 feet-- not 6 feet deep. It must have been 5 feet deep, 6 feet long, and 6 feet wide. So all of us fit in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could not stand up. The-- the best you could do is like stand on your knees halfway. You could not stand on your feet straight up, only bent down. And there was only one trap, one door, that you got in there. What Mr. Scastny did, he took out the dirt, put a floor on top, put straw on it. And he had holes in the walls now. So in case something happens, he'll let the pigs in from their adjoining rooms, running back and forth. Well, it never came to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where we spent, what, two months? I know we spent there the whole month of January, the two days in February, and part of December. If you ever been in a hole, this is it.
